name: CS-375 Logic, Proof & Computation
description: Propositional and predicate logic, SAT, resolution, natural deduction, model theory basics, and exam-style revision practice.

definitions.def: A mapping from a finite set of propositional variables to Boolean truth values {0,1}. It specifies the truth value of each atomic proposition.
A basic propositional symbol with no internal logical structure, representing an indivisible proposition.
A Binary Decision Diagram is a directed acyclic graph representing a Boolean function by branching on variable assignments.
A disjunction of Literals.
Conjunctive Normal Form is a conjunction of Clauses.
A proof system is complete if every semantically valid formula can be formally derived within the system.
If every finite subset of a set of formulas is satisfiable, then the entire set is satisfiable.
A binary connective (A ∧ B) that is true iff both operands are true.
A set of formulas is consistent if no Contradiction can be formally derived from it.
The false formula ⊥, or any formula false under every Assignment.
The maximum nesting level of logical connectives in a Formula.
A finite sequence of formulas where each follows from previous formulas by inference rules.
A binary connective (A ∨ B) true iff at least one operand is true.
Disjunctive Normal Form is a disjunction of conjunctive clauses of Literals.
Davis–Putnam–Logemann–Loveland algorithm: a proof/search procedure for deciding unsatisfiability of CNF formulas using splitting, unit propagation, reduction, and conflict detection.
Two formulas are equivalent if they have identical truth values under every Assignment (or every Structure in predicate logic).
A syntactically well-formed expression built recursively from Atoms using logical connectives (and quantifiers in predicate logic).
A Boolean function (f:{0,1}^n → {0,1}).
A formula in CNF where each Clause contains at most one positive Literal.
A connective (A → B), false only when A is true and B is false.
A proof principle establishing a property for all recursively defined objects by proving base and recursive cases.
Either an Atom or its Negation.
An interpretation under which a Formula evaluates true. In propositional logic this is an Assignment; in predicate logic a Structure.
Unary connective ¬A, true iff A is false.
A symbol denoting a relation over objects in a Structure.
A formula is in prenex normal form if all Quantifiers appear at the front, followed by a quantifier-free matrix.
Logical operators binding variables: universal (∀) and existential (∃).
An inference rule deriving a new Clause from two clauses containing complementary Literals.
The Clause produced by applying Resolution to two parent clauses.
The decision problem of determining whether a Formula is Satisfiable.
A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one Model making it true.
The formal meaning of logical expressions via interpretations assigning truth values.
An expression of the form Γ ⊢ A stating A is derivable from assumptions Γ.
A triple ((S,F,P)) specifying sorts, function symbols, and predicate symbols for predicate logic.
A proof system is sound if every derivable formula is semantically valid.
An interpretation assigning meanings to symbols of a Signature.
Replacing variables in a Formula with terms or formulas while preserving syntactic correctness.
The formal rules specifying which symbol strings are valid Formulas.
A propositional formula true under every Assignment.
A syntactic expression denoting an object in predicate logic, built from constants, variables, and function symbols.
A formula true in every Model (or every Structure).
A symbol standing for an arbitrary object (predicate logic) or truth-valued proposition (propositional logic).
A complete Derivation establishing a conclusion.
A formula logically implied by a set of premises.
A proof of unsatisfiability by deriving Contradiction.
A clause relation used in simplifying Resolution.
A Clause containing exactly one Literal.
In DPLL, detection of the empty clause showing unsatisfiability.

flashcards.q: Q1: What is the difference between syntax and semantics?
flashcards.a: Syntax: rules defining well-formed formulas. Concerned with form only. Semantics: assigns meaning/truth values to formulas under interpretations. Recursive syntax rules: atoms, ⊤, ⊥ are formulas; if A formula then ¬A formula; if A,B formulas then (A ∧ B), (A ∨ B), (A → B) formulas. Example: (A ∧ B) is syntactically valid, but semantically false if A=1 and B=0. Key idea: syntax = form, semantics = meaning.

flashcards.q: Q2: What is a formula and how is it recursively constructed?
flashcards.a: Recursive construction: 1. Base: every atomic proposition Pi, ⊤ and ⊥ are formulas. 2. If A formula then ¬A formula. 3. If A,B formulas then (A ∧ B), (A ∨ B), (A → B) are formulas. A formula is well-formed iff generated by finitely many applications of these rules. Recursive structure gives unique parse trees and allows structural induction.

flashcards.q: Q3: Explain why logical equivalence matters in Computer Science.
flashcards.a: A ≡ B iff both formulas have identical truth values under every assignment. Importance: formula simplification, circuit optimisation, SAT preprocessing, compiler optimisation, and program verification. Example equivalence: A → B ≡ ¬A ∨ B.

flashcards.q: Q4: What is the difference between: - Valid - Satisfiable - Unsatisfiable - Contradiction - Tautology
flashcards.a: Valid/Tautology: true under all assignments. Satisfiable: true under at least one assignment. Unsatisfiable: true under no assignments. Contradiction: always false, equivalent to ⊥. Every tautology is satisfiable, but not every satisfiable formula is valid.

flashcards.q: Q5: How would you justify whether this is true? "If (F) is satisfiable and (F → G) is satisfiable, then (G) is satisfiable."
flashcards.a: The statement is FALSE. Counterexample: let F=A and G=⊥. Then F is satisfiable. Also F → G = A → ⊥ ≡ ¬A, which is satisfiable. But G=⊥ is not satisfiable. Therefore the claim fails.

flashcards.q: Q6: Explain the relationship between validity and satisfiability.
flashcards.a: Key theorem: F valid ⇔ ¬F unsatisfiable. If F is true under every assignment, then ¬F is false under every assignment. Conversely, if ¬F has no satisfying assignment, then F must always be true.

flashcards.q: Q7: Why is every tautology satisfiable, but not every satisfiable formula valid? Give example of each.
flashcards.a: A tautology is true under every assignment, so it must be satisfiable. Example tautology: A ∨ ¬A. A satisfiable formula only needs one satisfying assignment, so it may fail under others. Example: A ∧ B is satisfiable but not valid.

flashcards.q: Q8: How would you distinguish CNF from DNF?
flashcards.a: CNF = conjunction of clauses, where each clause is a disjunction of literals. Example: (A ∨ ¬B) ∧ (¬A ∨ C). DNF = disjunction of conjunctions. Example: (A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ C). CNF has ∧ outside and ∨ inside. DNF has ∨ outside and ∧ inside. CNF is preferred for SAT solving; DNF makes satisfiability checking easy.

flashcards.q: Q9: Why is CNF preferred for SAT solving?
flashcards.a: Reasons: clause-based reasoning, resolution operates directly on clauses, and DPLL algorithms are designed for CNF input. CNF supports efficient propagation and conflict analysis.

flashcards.q: Q10: Why does converting CNF to DNF cause exponential blow-up? Mention distributivity explosion.
flashcards.a: Repeated distributivity expands every clause combination. A CNF with n clauses of size m may generate up to m^n conjunctions in DNF. This exponential growth is called distributivity explosion.

flashcards.q: Q11: What makes a Horn formula computationally special?
flashcards.a: A Horn clause has at most one positive literal. Example: ¬A ∨ ¬B ∨ C, equivalent to (A ∧ B) → C. Horn satisfiability is solvable in polynomial time and forms the basis of Prolog and logic programming.

flashcards.q: Q12: How would you prove resolution is sound?
flashcards.a: Assume the parent clauses are true under some assignment. If one clause contains A and another contains ¬A, then at least one of the remaining literals must be true. Therefore the resolvent must also be true. Hence every resolution step preserves logical consequence, so resolution is sound.

flashcards.q: Q13: How would you disprove a modified inference rule?
flashcards.a: Construct a countermodel: make all premises true while making the conclusion false. Example: from A ∨ B infer A. Countermodel: A=0, B=1. Premise true, conclusion false, so the rule is unsound.

flashcards.q: Q14: How do you build a resolution derivation of ⊥?
flashcards.a: Procedure: 1. Convert formulas to CNF. 2. Label clauses. 3. Resolve clauses containing complementary literals. 4. Continue until deriving the empty clause ⊥. Example chain: A, ¬A ⇒ ⊥.

flashcards.q: Q15: Why does deriving the empty clause prove unsatisfiability?
flashcards.a: The empty clause ⊥ represents falsehood and is unsatisfiable. If resolution derives ⊥ from a clause set F, then F logically entails contradiction. By soundness and refutation completeness, F has no satisfying assignment.

flashcards.q: Q16: Why can a CNF with at least one negative literal in every clause fail to derive ⊥?
flashcards.a: If every clause contains a negative literal, assign every variable false. Then each negative literal becomes true, so every clause is satisfied. Therefore the CNF is satisfiable, and sound resolution cannot derive ⊥.

flashcards.q: Q17: What is the difference between: - Resolution soundness - Resolution completeness - Refutation completeness
flashcards.a: Soundness: every derived clause is logically entailed. Completeness: every logically entailed clause can in principle be derived. Refutation completeness: if a clause set is unsatisfiable, resolution can derive ⊥.

flashcards.q: Q18: Describe DPLL step-by-step.
flashcards.a: Main DPLL steps: 1. Unit propagation. 2. Pure literal elimination. 3. Splitting/branching on variables. 4. Backtracking after conflicts. 5. Conflict detection. DPLL is sound and complete and underlies modern SAT solving.

flashcards.q: Q19: Resolution vs DPLL — compare.
flashcards.a: Resolution is a proof system producing derivations from clauses. DPLL is a search algorithm producing either a satisfying assignment or UNSAT result. Resolution is mainly theoretical; DPLL is the practical basis of SAT solvers. Both are sound and complete.

flashcards.q: Q20: Why is SAT NP-complete?
flashcards.a: SAT is in NP because a proposed assignment can be checked in polynomial time. SAT is NP-hard by the Cook-Levin theorem, which shows every NP problem can be reduced to SAT. Therefore SAT is NP-complete.

flashcards.q: Q21: Compare complexity of: - SAT - 2-SAT - 3-SAT - Horn-SAT
flashcards.a: SAT: NP-complete. 3-SAT: NP-complete. 2-SAT: solvable in polynomial time. Horn-SAT: solvable in polynomial time. Predicate satisfiability is undecidable.

flashcards.q: Q22: Why is 2-SAT polynomial but 3-SAT NP-complete?
flashcards.a: 2-SAT has restricted clause structure allowing polynomial-time graph algorithms and bounded resolution growth. 3-SAT is expressive enough to encode arbitrary computation, making it NP-complete.

flashcards.q: Q23: How would you prove a formula using natural deduction?
flashcards.a: Strategy: identify the main connective of the target formula, use the matching introduction rule, open assumptions when needed, derive intermediate results using elimination rules, then discharge assumptions correctly.

flashcards.q: Q24: When should you use: - ∧ introduction - ∨ elimination - → introduction - RAA - EFQ
flashcards.a: Use ∧ introduction when proving both conjuncts. Use ∨ elimination for proof by cases. Use → introduction by assuming the antecedent and deriving the consequent. Use RAA for contradiction proofs. Use EFQ once ⊥ has been derived.

flashcards.q: Q25: How would you prove implication (A → B)?
flashcards.a: Assume A, derive B, then discharge the assumption using implication introduction (→+).

flashcards.q: Q26: Why is assumption discharge essential? This tests real ND understanding.
flashcards.a: Discharge allows temporary assumptions to be removed from dependency lists. Without discharge, implication proofs and contradiction arguments would not work correctly. It enables conditional reasoning.

flashcards.q: Q27: Why is predicate logic more expressive than propositional logic?
flashcards.a: Predicate logic adds quantifiers, objects, functions, and relations. It can express general statements such as ∀x(P(x) → Q(x)), which propositional logic cannot represent.

flashcards.q: Q28: What is a signature and why is it needed?
flashcards.a: A signature Σ defines the vocabulary of the logic. It contains sorts, function symbols, and predicate symbols. Structures interpret symbols from the signature.

flashcards.q: Q29: What is the difference between: - Term - Formula - Predicate - Structure
flashcards.a: A term denotes an object. A formula denotes a truth value. A predicate is a relation symbol. A structure provides interpretations for constants, functions, and predicates over a domain.

flashcards.q: Q30: How would you formalise an English statement into predicate logic?
flashcards.a: Method: 1. Identify the domain. 2. Choose predicates/functions. 3. Translate quantifiers carefully. 4. Check scope and implication direction. Common mistake: using ∧ instead of → after ∀.

flashcards.q: Q31: How would you answer a bisimulation formalisation question?
flashcards.a: Define variables and relations carefully. Translate universal conditions with ∀ and existence conditions with ∃. Encode the forward and backward simulation conditions using implications and conjunctions.

flashcards.q: Q32: What are common quantifier mistakes? - Reversing ∀ and ∃ - Wrong scope - Missing implication
flashcards.a: Common mistakes: reversing ∀ and ∃, incorrect quantifier scope, missing implications after universal quantifiers, and using → instead of ∧ inside existential statements.

flashcards.q: Q33: What makes something a structure?
flashcards.a: A structure consists of carrier/domain sets together with interpretations for constants, functions, and predicates.

flashcards.q: Q34: How does model satisfaction differ between propositional and predicate logic?
flashcards.a: In propositional logic, satisfaction depends only on truth assignments to atoms. In predicate logic, satisfaction depends on a structure, domain, interpretations of symbols, and usually a variable assignment.

flashcards.q: Q35: State Gödel's Completeness Theorem.
flashcards.a: Gödel Completeness Theorem: Γ ⊨ A iff Γ ⊢ A. Every semantically valid formula is formally provable, and every provable formula is semantically valid.

flashcards.q: Q36: Why is completeness important for Computer Science?
flashcards.a: Completeness guarantees that all semantically correct results are derivable in the proof system. Applications include automated theorem proving, verification, proof assistants, and logical reasoning systems.

flashcards.q: Q37: Soundness vs completeness — explain clearly.
flashcards.a: Soundness means every provable statement is true. Completeness means every true statement is provable. Phrase to remember: soundness prevents proving falsehoods; completeness ensures all truths are provable.

flashcards.q: Q38: State the Compactness Theorem.
flashcards.a: Compactness Theorem: if every finite subset of Γ is satisfiable, then Γ itself is satisfiable. Equivalently, if Γ is unsatisfiable, some finite subset of Γ is already unsatisfiable.

flashcards.q: Q39: Why does compactness matter?
flashcards.a: Compactness reduces infinite logical reasoning to finite subsets. It is important for existence proofs, decidability arguments, model theory, and understanding logical expressiveness limitations.

flashcards.q: Q40: How does compactness relate to completeness?
flashcards.a: Completeness and compactness are closely linked. Compactness can be proved using completeness: if Γ were unsatisfiable, completeness gives a finite proof of contradiction using finitely many premises.

flashcards.q: Q41: Briefly discuss satisfiability problems for propositional and predicate logic regarding decidability and complexity.
flashcards.a: Propositional SAT is decidable and NP-complete. 2-SAT is polynomial-time solvable. 3-SAT is NP-complete. Horn-SAT is polynomial-time solvable. Predicate satisfiability is undecidable.

flashcards.q: Q42: Why is predicate satisfiability undecidable?
flashcards.a: Predicate logic is expressive enough to encode Turing machine computations and the halting problem. Therefore no algorithm can decide satisfiability for all predicate formulas.

flashcards.q: Q43: Why do stronger logics often lose decidability?
flashcards.a: Greater expressive power allows stronger logics to encode richer computational behaviour and unbounded reasoning. This extra expressiveness often leads to undecidability.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A mapping from a finite set of propositional variables to Boolean truth values {0,1}. It specifies the truth value of each atomic proposition.
quiz.explanation: Assignment: A mapping from a finite set of propositional variables to Boolean truth values {0,1}. It specifies the truth value of each atomic proposition.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A basic propositional symbol with no internal logical structure, representing an indivisible proposition.
quiz.explanation: Atom: A basic propositional symbol with no internal logical structure, representing an indivisible proposition.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A Binary Decision Diagram is a directed acyclic graph representing a Boolean function by branching on variable assignments.
quiz.explanation: BDD: A Binary Decision Diagram is a directed acyclic graph representing a Boolean function by branching on variable assignments.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A disjunction of Literals.
quiz.explanation: Clause: A disjunction of Literals.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Conjunctive Normal Form is a conjunction of Clauses.
quiz.explanation: CNF: Conjunctive Normal Form is a conjunction of Clauses.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A proof system is complete if every semantically valid formula can be formally derived within the system.
quiz.explanation: Completeness: A proof system is complete if every semantically valid formula can be formally derived within the system.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: If every finite subset of a set of formulas is satisfiable, then the entire set is satisfiable.
quiz.explanation: Compactness: If every finite subset of a set of formulas is satisfiable, then the entire set is satisfiable.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A binary connective (A ∧ B) that is true iff both operands are true.
quiz.explanation: Conjunction: A binary connective (A ∧ B) that is true iff both operands are true.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A set of formulas is consistent if no Contradiction can be formally derived from it.
quiz.explanation: Consistency: A set of formulas is consistent if no Contradiction can be formally derived from it.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The false formula ⊥, or any formula false under every Assignment.
quiz.explanation: Contradiction: The false formula ⊥, or any formula false under every Assignment.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The maximum nesting level of logical connectives in a Formula.
quiz.explanation: Depth: The maximum nesting level of logical connectives in a Formula.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A finite sequence of formulas where each follows from previous formulas by inference rules.
quiz.explanation: Derivation: A finite sequence of formulas where each follows from previous formulas by inference rules.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A binary connective (A ∨ B) true iff at least one operand is true.
quiz.explanation: Disjunction: A binary connective (A ∨ B) true iff at least one operand is true.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Disjunctive Normal Form is a disjunction of conjunctive clauses of Literals.
quiz.explanation: DNF: Disjunctive Normal Form is a disjunction of conjunctive clauses of Literals.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Davis–Putnam–Logemann–Loveland algorithm: a proof/search procedure for deciding unsatisfiability of CNF formulas using splitting, unit propagation, reduction, and conflict detection.
quiz.explanation: DPLL: Davis–Putnam–Logemann–Loveland algorithm: a proof/search procedure for deciding unsatisfiability of CNF formulas using splitting, unit propagation, reduction, and conflict detection.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Two formulas are equivalent if they have identical truth values under every Assignment (or every Structure in predicate logic).
quiz.explanation: Equivalence: Two formulas are equivalent if they have identical truth values under every Assignment (or every Structure in predicate logic).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A syntactically well-formed expression built recursively from Atoms using logical connectives (and quantifiers in predicate logic).
quiz.explanation: Formula: A syntactically well-formed expression built recursively from Atoms using logical connectives (and quantifiers in predicate logic).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A Boolean function (f:{0,1}^n → {0,1}).
quiz.explanation: Gate: A Boolean function (f:{0,1}^n → {0,1}).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A formula in CNF where each Clause contains at most one positive Literal.
quiz.explanation: Horn: A formula in CNF where each Clause contains at most one positive Literal.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A connective (A → B), false only when A is true and B is false.
quiz.explanation: Implication: A connective (A → B), false only when A is true and B is false.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A proof principle establishing a property for all recursively defined objects by proving base and recursive cases.
quiz.explanation: Induction: A proof principle establishing a property for all recursively defined objects by proving base and recursive cases.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Either an Atom or its Negation.
quiz.explanation: Literal: Either an Atom or its Negation.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: An interpretation under which a Formula evaluates true. In propositional logic this is an Assignment; in predicate logic a Structure.
quiz.explanation: Model: An interpretation under which a Formula evaluates true. In propositional logic this is an Assignment; in predicate logic a Structure.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Unary connective ¬A, true iff A is false.
quiz.explanation: Negation: Unary connective ¬A, true iff A is false.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A symbol denoting a relation over objects in a Structure.
quiz.explanation: Predicate: A symbol denoting a relation over objects in a Structure.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A formula is in prenex normal form if all Quantifiers appear at the front, followed by a quantifier-free matrix.
quiz.explanation: Prenex: A formula is in prenex normal form if all Quantifiers appear at the front, followed by a quantifier-free matrix.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Logical operators binding variables: universal (∀) and existential (∃).
quiz.explanation: Quantifier: Logical operators binding variables: universal (∀) and existential (∃).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: An inference rule deriving a new Clause from two clauses containing complementary Literals.
quiz.explanation: Resolution: An inference rule deriving a new Clause from two clauses containing complementary Literals.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The Clause produced by applying Resolution to two parent clauses.
quiz.explanation: Resolvent: The Clause produced by applying Resolution to two parent clauses.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The decision problem of determining whether a Formula is Satisfiable.
quiz.explanation: SAT: The decision problem of determining whether a Formula is Satisfiable.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one Model making it true.
quiz.explanation: Satisfiability: A formula is satisfiable if there exists at least one Model making it true.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The formal meaning of logical expressions via interpretations assigning truth values.
quiz.explanation: Semantics: The formal meaning of logical expressions via interpretations assigning truth values.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: An expression of the form Γ ⊢ A stating A is derivable from assumptions Γ.
quiz.explanation: Sequent: An expression of the form Γ ⊢ A stating A is derivable from assumptions Γ.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A triple ((S,F,P)) specifying sorts, function symbols, and predicate symbols for predicate logic.
quiz.explanation: Signature: A triple ((S,F,P)) specifying sorts, function symbols, and predicate symbols for predicate logic.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A proof system is sound if every derivable formula is semantically valid.
quiz.explanation: Soundness: A proof system is sound if every derivable formula is semantically valid.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: An interpretation assigning meanings to symbols of a Signature.
quiz.explanation: Structure: An interpretation assigning meanings to symbols of a Signature.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: Replacing variables in a Formula with terms or formulas while preserving syntactic correctness.
quiz.explanation: Substitution: Replacing variables in a Formula with terms or formulas while preserving syntactic correctness.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: The formal rules specifying which symbol strings are valid Formulas.
quiz.explanation: Syntax: The formal rules specifying which symbol strings are valid Formulas.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A propositional formula true under every Assignment.
quiz.explanation: Tautology: A propositional formula true under every Assignment.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A syntactic expression denoting an object in predicate logic, built from constants, variables, and function symbols.
quiz.explanation: Term: A syntactic expression denoting an object in predicate logic, built from constants, variables, and function symbols.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A formula true in every Model (or every Structure).
quiz.explanation: Validity: A formula true in every Model (or every Structure).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A symbol standing for an arbitrary object (predicate logic) or truth-valued proposition (propositional logic).
quiz.explanation: Variable: A symbol standing for an arbitrary object (predicate logic) or truth-valued proposition (propositional logic).

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A complete Derivation establishing a conclusion.
quiz.explanation: Proof: A complete Derivation establishing a conclusion.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A formula logically implied by a set of premises.
quiz.explanation: Consequence: A formula logically implied by a set of premises.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A proof of unsatisfiability by deriving Contradiction.
quiz.explanation: Refutation: A proof of unsatisfiability by deriving Contradiction.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A clause relation used in simplifying Resolution.
quiz.explanation: Subsumption: A clause relation used in simplifying Resolution.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: A Clause containing exactly one Literal.
quiz.explanation: Unit: A Clause containing exactly one Literal.

quiz.question: Which term matches this definition: In DPLL, detection of the empty clause showing unsatisfiability.
quiz.explanation: Conflict: In DPLL, detection of the empty clause showing unsatisfiability.

quickReference.value: F is valid iff not-F is unsatisfiable
Semantic validity aligns with formal derivability

examTraps: Valid means true in all models; satisfiable means true in at least one model.
Resolution soundness and refutation completeness are different claims.
2-SAT is polynomial, but 3-SAT and general SAT are NP-complete.
Quantifier scope mistakes can completely change a formalisation.
Soundness prevents false derivations; completeness prevents missing valid truths.
